source_paper_title: Tight Running Times for Minimum ℓq-Norm Load Balancing: Beyond Exponential Dependencies on 1/ϵ
context:
Page 2, Introduction (Contribution Overview); also reiterated on page 46, Section H (Conclusion)

It remains an problem to obtain tight running times when m = \(\Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)\) for \(\theta \in (1/2, 1]\).
title: Tight dependence on \(\varepsilon\) for PTAS/FPTAS of \(P\|\|\sum_i C_i^q\) when \(m=\Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)\) with \(\theta\in(1/2,1]\)
latex:
Given a constant \(q>1\), consider the identical-machines scheduling problem \(P\|\|\sum_{i=1}^m C_i^q\):
- The input consists of an integer \(m\ge 1\) (number of identical machines) and \(n\ge 1\) jobs with processing times \(p_1,\dots,p_n\in\mathbb{R}_{>0}\).
- A schedule is an assignment of each job to one machine. For a schedule, let \(C_i=\sum_{j\text{ assigned to machine }i} p_j\) be the load of machine \(i\).
- The objective value of the schedule is \(\sum_{i=1}^m C_i^q\). Let \(\mathrm{OPT}(I)\) be the minimum objective value over all schedules for an instance \(I\).

Fix any \(\theta\in(1/2,1]\). For each \(\varepsilon\in(0,1)\), restrict attention to instances \(I\) for which the number of machines satisfies \(m=\Theta\big((1/\varepsilon)^\theta\big)\) (i.e., there exist absolute constants \(0<c_1\le c_2\) independent of \(\varepsilon\) such that \(c_1(1/\varepsilon)^\theta\le m\le c_2(1/\varepsilon)^\theta\)).

Determine the optimal (tight) asymptotic running time dependence on \(\varepsilon\) that is achievable by a \((1+\varepsilon)\)-approximation algorithm for \(P\|\|\sum_i C_i^q\) on this restricted family of instances.

Concretely, the open task is to establish matching upper and lower bounds (e.g., under ETH for lower bounds) for the smallest function \(T(\varepsilon)\) such that there exists an algorithm which, for every such instance \(I\), returns a schedule of cost at most \((1+\varepsilon)\mathrm{OPT}(I)\) in time \(T(\varepsilon)\cdot (n+m)^{O(1)}\). In particular, decide whether an FPTAS (i.e., \(T(\varepsilon)=\mathrm{poly}(1/\varepsilon)\)) exists in this regime or whether a subexponential/exponential dependence on \(1/\varepsilon\) is necessary, and if so, what the tight exponent is as a function of \(\theta\).
location_in_paper: Page 2, Introduction (Contribution Overview); also reiterated on page 46, Section H (Conclusion)
open_problem_quote: It remains an problem to obtain tight running times when m = \(\Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)\) for \(\theta \in (1/2, 1]\).
